visual_notes: Pyrite is mineralogy's great impersonator — 'fool's gold' for millennia. The striated faces of pyrite cubes (from oscillation between cube and pyritohedron) are diagnostic. Iron cross twins — two interlocking pyritohedra — are among mineralogy's most architecturally perfect specimens. Pyrite suns (flat radiating discs) are unique and immediately recognizable forms.
prompt_fragments: perfect Navajún pyrite cube — striated brassy faces of absolute geometric order
iron cross twin: two pyritohedra intersecting at 90° — mineralogy's signature form
pyrite sun: flat radiating disc of brassy crystals from Illinois coal seam
vibe_notes: Pyrite is the great deceiver — the mineral that convinced explorers they'd found gold and sent fleets home to disgrace. But a perfect Navajún pyrite cube is not deception — it is geometric perfection in the service of iron sulfide chemistry. Iron cross twins are architecture. Pyrite suns are geology's mandalas.
folklore_summary: Pyrite has been 'fool's gold' since antiquity — its brass-yellow metallic luster has deceived prospectors, miners, and explorers throughout history. Captain Martin Frobisher returned from his 1576 Arctic expedition with 200 tonnes of 'ore' that turned out to be pyrite — the English Crown had funded
